ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for gyudon (Japanese beef rice bowl). FOODON_00002572 (beef dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists for gyudon as a composite dish. A0CKW (Beef, cooked) is used as the closest parent anchor for the dominant protein component. The dish also contains rice, daikon, and ponzu sauce.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for gyudon or Japanese beef rice bowl as a specific food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of gyudon is approximately 400 g, comprising a bowl of steamed rice topped with simmered beef and onion, garnished with diced and grated daikon, and drizzled with ponzu sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Gyudon (beef bowl) — composite estimate based on USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked beef (NDB 13397), cooked short-grain white rice (NDB 20444), daikon radish raw (NDB 11429), and ponzu sauce (USDA Branded Foods reference); proportions estimated at approximately 35% beef, 45% rice, 12% daikon, 8% ponzu and seasoning by weight.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for gyudon with daikon and ponzu as a composite dish. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries for cooked beef, steamed white rice, raw daikon radish, and ponzu-style sauce, weighted by approximate serving proportions (beef 35%, rice 45%, daikon 12%, ponzu/seasoning 8%). This is a medium-confidence composite estimate. HPB Singapore Foodo and FAO/INFOODS SEA do not carry a gyudon entry. ICMR-NIN and IMR Malaysia MyFCD are not applicable for Japanese cuisine.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (Japanese short-grain, cooked) GI approximately 54; composite dish GI estimated using rice as dominant glycemic contributor weighted by carbohydrate proportion.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for gyudon with daikon and ponzu. GI is estimated using Japanese short-grain white rice (GI ~54, low-medium boundary, University of Sydney GI Database) as the dominant glycemic carbohydrate contributor. Beef, daikon, and ponzu sauce contribute negligible glycemic load. The composite dish GI is estimated at 54 (low category). Glycemic load per 400 g serving calculated as: (54 × 63.2 g carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 34.1.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce, dashi, and ponzu sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid gyudon with ponzu sauce due to its high sodium content (approximately 310 mg per 100 g, equating to ~1240 mg per standard 400 g serving), which represents more than half the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced-sodium soy sauce or ponzu and limiting portion size are advisable strategies.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that a standard 400 g serving of gyudon carries a glycemic load of approximately 34, driven primarily by the steamed white rice component; portion control, substituting with brown rice where available, and pairing with the daikon and protein-rich beef to slow gastric emptying can help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef and moderate sodium from seasoning sauces
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease should consume gyudon in moderation given its saturated fat content from thinly sliced beef (approximately 1.5 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium from ponzu and soy-based seasoning; the daikon component provides some beneficial phytonutrients and vitamin C, but overall the dish warrants portion-size awareness for those on heart-healthy dietary plans.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Diced and Grated Daikon Gyudon with Ponzu Sauce is a Japanese beef rice bowl (gyudon) variant served at Sukiya, a major Japanese fast-food chain, and available in Singapore via field survey. The dish consists of a base of steamed Japanese short-grain rice topped with thinly sliced beef and onion that have been simmered in a sweet-savoury dashi, soy sauce, mirin, and sake broth. The topping is garnished with two forms of daikon radish: finely diced cubes for texture and freshly grated daikon (daikon oroshi) for a cooling, slightly peppery contrast. The entire bowl is finished with a drizzle of ponzu sauce, a citrus-based Japanese condiment made from yuzu or sudachi juice blended with soy sauce, mirin, and dashi, which imparts a bright, tangy, umami-rich flavour that lightens the richness of the beef. The texture profile combines tender, juicy beef with soft rice, crisp daikon cubes, and the silky, moist quality of grated daikon. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate protein from beef (approximately 8.2 g per 100 g), moderate carbohydrate from rice (approximately 15.8 g per 100 g), and is relatively low in fat. The sodium content is elevated due to the soy-based seasoning and ponzu sauce, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at 54 (low-medium boundary) based on the white rice component. Clinically, the dish is moderate in glycemic load per serving and should be consumed with portion awareness by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease. Daikon radish contributes dietary fibre, vitamin C, and digestive enzymes (diastase), which may aid digestion. This dish is suitable for non-vegetarian, dairy-free diets but contains soy and wheat allergens from the soy sauce and ponzu.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for gyudon with daikon and ponzu as a composite dish in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries for cooked beef, steamed white rice, raw daikon, and ponzu-style sauce weighted by approximate serving proportions. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using Japanese short-grain white rice as the dominant glycemic contributor. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central component entries; no single approved-source entry for gyudon with daikon and ponzu exists in any primary regional database. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entries used for this composite estimate and is not expected to be a significant contributor in this dish. 3. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and carry medium confidence. 4. The food_group in the input JSON was listed as 'Oils, seasonings and condiments', which is incorrect for a beef rice bowl; this has been corrected to 'Mixed' as the dish is a composite of rice, beef, vegetables, and condiment sauce. 5. GI is estimated at 54 using Japanese short-grain white rice (University of Sydney GI Database) as the dominant glycemic carbohydrate contributor; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found. 6. Glycemic load per 400 g serving = (54 × 63.2) / 100 = 34.1. 7. The dish contains soy and wheat allergens from soy sauce and ponzu; halal status is unconfirmed as the beef sourcing at Sukiya Singapore is not certified halal. 8. All -1 sentinel values represent nutrients not reported in the source database entries used for this composite estimate.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected food_group from 'Oils, seasonings and condiments' (incorrect input value) to 'Mixed' to accurately reflect the composite nature of the dish.
Constructed composite nutrient estimates from USDA FoodData Central component entries for cooked beef, steamed white rice, raw daikon, and ponzu sauce.
